reason: PCI is the expansion bus bridged off the processor/host bus to extend connectivity to peripherals; SCSI is a device bus, controllers/multiple-bus are not connectivity extensions of the processor bus.
IBM's 16-bit bus for the PC/AT is the Industry Standard Architecture (ISA) bus.

Monitor attaches through a video adapter card sitting on the PCI/AGP expansion bus; SCSI is storage/scanner, memory bus and Rambus are DRAM interfaces. Item is poorly posed and the common textbook key for it is 'SCSI bus', which is technically wrong.
In Hamacher's single-bus datapath the Z register only latches the ALU output and drives the bus one way (out); PC/IR/Temp are read and written over the bus.
The grouped general-purpose registers with multiple read/write ports in a multi-bus organisation are called the register file.
Multiple buses let several operand transfers happen in parallel, so an instruction completes in fewer clock cycles/steps.
ISA slots carried disk (IDE/MFM) controller cards; RAM uses the memory bus, and GPU/optical drives are the later PCI/IDE era. Weak legacy item but hard disk is the intended link.
CPU = ALU + control unit + registers; RAM and cache are outside the CPU core definition.
The CPU performs the computation; RAM stores and input only supplies data.
The CPU is conventionally called the brain of the computer (ALU and CU are its parts, not the whole).
Secondary memory (disk/SSD) is non-volatile permanent storage; registers and main memory are volatile.
The motherboard is the main circuit board of the system unit.
CPU socket and memory slots are mounted on the motherboard.
Motherboard is commonly called the main printed circuit board (PCB) / mainboard; the other names are not real terms.
Turning machine-form results into human-readable form is the output unit/interface's job.

Textbook (Hamacher) phrasing: the control unit generates the timing signals that govern all transfers. But 'Control signals' is also literally correct, so the item has two defensible answers and needs human adjudication.
In the classic single-bus structure all units including I/O devices hang off one common bus; star bus and Rambus are not I/O bus structures. Item is loosely worded.
The control unit fetches and decodes instructions and issues control signals; logic ops are the ALU's, storage is memory's.
A step-by-step problem-solving procedure is by definition an algorithm.
EEPROM = Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory.
